title: Roast Spatchcock Chicken
subtitle: Flattened bird, faster and more evenly cooked
excerpt: Removing the backbone and pressing the chicken flat before roasting solves the classic problem of an uneven bird: the breast overcooks before the legs are done. A spatchcocked chicken roasts in 45 minutes at 220°C and comes out with evenly cooked meat and properly blistered skin. A weeknight roast that eats like a Sunday one.
sourceNotes: Traditional spatchcocking technique documented in British and American cookery from the eighteenth century. The term appears in various forms from at least 1785. Adapted from standard roasting guidelines for flattened poultry.

content.text: a chicken takes about three minutes and addresses one of roasting's chronic problems: a standard chicken has two very different muscle groups at very different distances from the bone. The breast cooks in 35 minutes at high heat; the thigh joint needs 50. Flattening the bird brings both into roughly the same plane so the oven reaches them evenly.

The other benefit is skin. A flat bird presents almost all of its skin to the oven's radiant heat at once. The result is crispness across the breast, thighs, and drumsticks, not just the top surface. 45 minutes at 220°C is generally enough for a 1.5 kg bird. Check the thigh joint with a probe: 75°C means it is done.

Heavy kitchen scissors are the right tool for cutting through the backbone. The spine is cartilaginous rather than solid bone at the rib attachment points, so scissors manage it without difficulty. A large roasting pan or flat baking tray with enough room to hold the flattened bird without overcrowding.

Place the chicken breast-side down on a board. Using kitchen scissors, cut down each side of the backbone from tail to neck and remove it entirely (freeze for stock). Flip the bird over, place both palms flat on the breastbone, and press firmly until you hear and feel the breastbone crack flat. The bird should now lie completely flat.

Mix {{olive-oil}} of olive oil with the grated {{garlic}} of garlic, lemon zest, and thyme leaves if using. Rub the mixture all over both sides of the flattened bird. Season generously with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}, working the salt under the skin of the breast where possible.

Heat the oven to 220°C (200°C fan). Place the bird skin-side up in the roasting pan. Roast for 40 to 45 minutes until the skin is deeply golden all over and the thickest part of the thigh reads 75°C on a probe. Rest for 10 minutes before carving. Cut along the breastbone to split into two halves, then separate legs from breasts.

rows.fix: Use both fists on the breastbone rather than flat palms, and press in a sharp downward motion. A very large bird may need the breastbone scored with a knife first.
rows.cause: The crack needs more force or the cartilage is particularly tough

rows.fix: Pat the skin completely dry with kitchen paper. Cold, damp skin steams rather than crisps.
rows.cause: Bird went in straight from the fridge or skin was not dried
rows.symptom: Skin not crisping despite the high temperature

rows.fix: Cover the breast loosely with foil and continue roasting the thigh to temperature.
rows.cause: Bird is unusually large or the thigh is a particularly thick joint
rows.symptom: Thigh not reaching 75°C while breast is already at 80°C

content.text: Spatchcocking is an old technique with a name of uncertain origin. The most likely theory traces it to an eighteenth-century Irish expression for a quickly prepared bird. The method was common in British cookery books through the Victorian era and fell out of fashion as the whole roast bird became the standard. It came back as a weeknight method in the food press from the 1990s onward, promoted as a faster way to roast a chicken. Serve with roast potatoes or a green salad and a sharp salsa verde.
